narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 43, first-year decile rank of 1st out of 10 in your school (well below the average of around 6th), third-year decile rank also 1st (again below the typical 6th), middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 48.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction firmly toward expecting you to pass since your 43 is notably higher than the average of 36.7 seen in past students. However, your third-year decile rank was the next most influential factor, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure because landing in the bottom 10% of your school that year signals weaker performance compared to peers who typically rank around the middle of their classes. Your first-year decile rank, also in the bottom 10% versus the usual middle ranking, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic strength. While your full-time status aligns with most admitted students (over 90%), and your middle-income background is common, it was your early low class rankings that overshadowed your solid LSAT, tipping the overall balance against you. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades place you in the lower tail of the distribution, where failure rates climb despite stronger test scores like yours. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant with clear drive, but a data-driven flag on early academic hurdles that often predict bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through targeted tutoring or a post-baccalaureate option, then reapply with updated transcripts showing improvement. You could also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering weak areas or explore bridge programs designed for students with your profile. With your standout LSAT as a foundation, focused efforts on grades could flip this prediction in your favor next time.
